nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, well-protected coastal bays; commonly found hiding under jetties and in dark areas; also in inshore rocky zones and among branching coral.

diet.notes: Has been observed readily accepting meaty frozen and prepared foods including enriched brine shrimp, enriched mysis shrimp, marine pellets, and flake foods.

summary: A small, slender, mostly-black reef fish with large dark eyes. Generally considered a peaceful, hardy beginner-level species well-suited to reef aquariums; tends to shelter among rockwork, sea urchins, or seagrass.
careNotes: Black Cardinalfish tend to hide among rockwork, sea urchins, long-spined urchins, or seagrass beds in the aquarium, so ample sheltered areas are generally advised. Groups have been reported to establish a loose hierarchy without notable aggression toward one another. LiveAquaria recommends a 30-gallon (approximately 113 L) minimum; this entry rounds up to 120 L to accommodate a small group. Temperature data shows a notable disagreement between sources: LiveAquaria recommends 22–26°C (72–78°F) while FishBase reports wild-habitat preference of 27–29°C; this entry uses the averaged range of approximately 24–27.5°C — keeper discretion is advised toward the upper end. The species is a paternal mouthbrooder with distinct pairing behavior during courtship and spawning, which has occasionally been replicated in captivity.
breedingNotes: Has been documented as a paternal mouthbrooder exhibiting distinct pairing during courtship and spawning. Captive breeding is rarely reported in detail but is considered possible in a species-appropriate aquarium.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, native range, temperature, taxonomy (order Kurtiformes), and habitat. Temperature disagreement noted: FishBase reports preferred wild habitat temperature of 27.1–29.3°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C). Averaged range used; dataStatus set to needs_review due to this spread.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder with distinct pairing during courtship and spawning. Rarely bred in home aquaria.
